name: Tanngrisnir
description: Tanngrisnir, meaning 'teeth-gnasher,' is one of the two goats that pull the chariot of the thunder god Thor in Norse mythology. Along with his brother Tanngnjóstr ('teeth-grinder'), Tanngrisnir is not merely an animal companion but a crucial element of Thor's power and travel. These goats possess the remarkable ability to be slaughtered, cooked, and eaten by Thor and his companions, only to be resurrected the next day, bone for bone. This regenerative power is vital for Thor's long journeys across Midgard, providing a reliable source of sustenance. However, the resurrection is contingent upon all the bones remaining intact. In one famous tale, a farmer's son, Þjálfi, breaks one of Tanngrisnir's leg bones to suck out the marrow. This act of disrespect results in the goat being lame upon resurrection, and Thor takes Þjálfi and his sister Röskva as his servants in compensation. This story highlights the importance of respecting the divine and the consequences of disrespecting even seemingly 'lesser' beings associated with the gods. Tanngrisnir represents not only Thor's power and ability to travel but also themes of sacrifice, renewal, and the delicate balance between mortals and the divine.
subtitle: Thor's regenerative chariot-pulling goat

keyMyths.title: Thor's Journey to Utgard
keyMyths.description: Thor travels to Utgard with Loki, Þjálfi, and Röskva, relying on Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr for transport and sustenance. The goats are slaughtered and eaten each night, then resurrected the following morning.

keyMyths.title: Þjálfi Breaks the Bone
keyMyths.description: During the journey to Utgard, Þjálfi breaks one of Tanngrisnir's leg bones to suck out the marrow, causing the goat to be lame upon resurrection. Thor punishes Þjálfi by taking him into service.

keyMyths.title: Resurrection Ritual
keyMyths.description: The resurrection of Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr requires all bones to be intact and placed properly. This highlights the importance of respect and proper conduct when dealing with divine beings and their associated creatures.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including tales of Thor and his goats.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, including tales of Thor and his goats.

sources.author: Hilda Ellis Davidson
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology and religion.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology and religion.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr appear in modern fiction, games, and art, often symbolizing resilience, regeneration, and the raw power of nature. They represent Thor's connection to the natural world and his ability to overcome challenges.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The precise origins of Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr are not explicitly detailed in surviving Norse texts. They appear as fully formed characters, intrinsically linked to Thor. Their existence is accepted as part of the established mythological landscape. Some scholars suggest that the goats represent the untamed, primal forces of nature harnessed by Thor. The goats' ability to be consumed and reborn may symbolize the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, a prominent theme in Norse paganism. Their names, 'teeth-gnasher' and 'teeth-grinder,' evoke a sense of raw, untamed power, reflecting Thor's own association with strength and the natural world. Their association with Thor is immediate and unquestioned.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent story featuring Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr involves Thor's journey to Útgarða-Loki's hall. During this trip, Thor and Loki stayed at a peasant's farm. Thor slaughtered the goats, cooked them, and shared the meal with the family. He warned them not to break the bones. However, Þjálfi, the farmer's son, disobeyed and cracked one of the bones to suck out the marrow. The next morning, Thor resurrected the goats, but one limped. Enraged, Thor demanded Þjálfi and his sister Röskva become his servants as compensation. This tale highlights the consequences of disrespecting the natural order and the importance of honoring agreements. The goats serve not only as transportation but as a source of sustenance for Thor and his companions.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr, while not directly worshipped, hold a significant place in Norse mythology as symbols of Thor's power and connection to the natural world. Their ability to be resurrected after being consumed underscores themes of renewal and cyclical existence, reflecting the Norse worldview. In modern interpretations, they represent resilience, the untamed power of nature, and the interconnectedness of life and death. They appear in contemporary art, literature, and games, often embodying the strength and endurance associated with the Norse gods. Their presence serves as a reminder of the deep respect for nature that permeated Norse culture.

shortDescription: Tanngrisnir is one of Thor's goats in Norse mythology, pulling his chariot and capable of being eaten and reborn, symbolizing renewal and Thor's might.
